cards.text: Pass by const reference syntax: {{c1::void func(const int& x)}}. This avoids {{c2::copying}} and prevents {{c3::modification}}.

cards.front: When should you prefer pass by const reference over pass by non-const reference?
cards.back: Favor pass by const reference in most cases, unless you specifically need the function to modify the argument. It provides safety while maintaining efficiency.

cards.question: Which function calls are valid?
cards.code:
void print(const int& x) { std::cout << x; }

int a = 5;
const int b = 10;
print(a); // 1
print(b); // 2
print(15); // 3
cards.explanation: Const references can bind to modifiable lvalues (a), non-modifiable lvalues (b), and rvalues (15).

cards.front: What two benefits does pass by const reference provide?
cards.back:
1) Avoids making a copy of the argument (efficient for large objects)
2) Guarantees that the function cannot modify the value being referenced (safe)

cards.text: For {{c1::expensive-to-copy}} types (like std::string or std::vector), favor passing by {{c2::const reference}} over passing by value when the function doesn't need to modify the argument.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot modify a value through a const reference. The parameter is const, so x cannot be incremented.
cards.explanation: Const references are read-only. To modify the parameter, use a non-const reference.

cards.front: Should you pass fundamental types (int, double, char) by const reference?
cards.back: No. For fundamental types, pass by value is typically as fast or faster than pass by const reference. Use pass by const reference for expensive-to-copy types.

cards.concept: When to use pass by const reference
cards.explanation:
Use pass by const reference when:
  • Passing expensive-to-copy types (strings, vectors, classes)
  • You don't need to modify the argument
  • You want to accept both lvalues and rvalues
Don't use for fundamental types - use pass by value instead.
examples: void print(const std::string& s);     // Good: avoid copy
void process(const std::vector<int>& v);  // Good: avoid copy
void calc(const int& x);              // Bad: just use int x
void func(int x);                     // Good: fundamentals by value

cards.text: Pass by const reference is ideal for {{c1::expensive-to-copy}} types like std::{{c2::string}} and std::{{c3::vector}}.
